model_narrative: We run a distribution operation that ships a product from a set of plants to a set of markets. For every plant and market we decide how much to ship along that route. Each plant can only supply so much, each market needs a certain amount delivered, and every route carries a known per-case shipping cost. The objective is to make the total transportation cost across all routes as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the canning plants where product is produced and shipped from

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the markets where product is demanded and shipped to

params.name: a
params.doc: the production capacity available at each plant, in cases

params.name: b
params.doc: the quantity required at each market, in cases

params.name: c
params.doc: the transport cost of shipping from a plant to a market, in thousands of dollars per case

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the amount shipped from each plant to each market, in cases

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all plant-market shipments, in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: For each plant, the total amount shipped out of that plant to all markets cannot exceed the production capacity available at that plant.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: For each market, the total amount shipped into that market from all plants must be at least the quantity required at that market.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost is the sum over every plant and market route of the amount shipped along that route valued at its per-case shipping cost. Set the total cost equal to this sum.

query: We're looking at leaning on the San Diego brewery to anchor the two eastern accounts. The proposal is that San Diego's combined shipments into New York and Chicago come to at least 400 kegs. Plug that in and walk me through the cost and the routing.

query: There's a proposal on the table to second-source the Topeka account so it isn't fed by a single brewery. We'd want Seattle's shipments into Topeka to be at least as large as San Diego's into Topeka. What does the optimizer return for the distribution bill and the lane mix?

query: Engineering is pitching a two-part rework of the southern flows: hold the San Diego-to-Topeka run to 200 kegs to ease the drivers, and at the same time trim Seattle's total outbound to 320 kegs while a fermenter is down for service. Run both together and walk me through where the cost and routing land.
expected_pyomo:
model.sd_top_cap = Constraint(expr=model.x['san-diego', 'topeka'] <= 200)
model.sea_load_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['seattle', j] for j in model.j) <= 320)

query: Seattle barely touches the eastern accounts — almost everything it brews goes to Chicago. Shouldn't Seattle be putting at least 150 kegs combined into New York and Topeka so it's not a one-hub line? What's keeping the model from spreading Seattle's output that way?

query: San Diego is hauling the lion's share of the New York order while Seattle sits back. Wouldn't it be more balanced to keep San Diego's New York volume to no more than half of the total New York deliveries — that is, half the sum of what Seattle and San Diego both send into New York? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: Half of our brewery-hub lanes are bone dry while a couple carry everything. Shouldn't every brewery keep a standing presence on every hub — say at least 50 kegs on each lane so no account is single-sourced? Walk me through what's pushing the plan the other way.

query: Some lanes are carrying huge keg volumes. Couldn't we just cap every brewery-to-hub lane at 225 kegs for a more balanced network? What's the tradeoff I'm missing?
